blocks.text: Chapter 2.
Identifying and Targeting the Right Customer
The ideas in 30 seconds
Layer two targeting constructs: the Ideal Customer Profile (the account that gets the most value and can buy) and the buyer persona (the individual decision-maker inside it).
The discipline pays: 71% of companies that exceed revenue goals maintain documented buyer personas, and 86% of business buyers are more likely to buy from sellers who understand their goals.
Jobs to Be Done: customers "hire" a product to do a job — target the job, not the demographic.
Intent data — first-party and anonymised signals of purchase interest — lets you reach in-market accounts before they self-identify.

Every growth strategy begins with a decision about who to serve. Two complementary frameworks dominate modern practice, and the strongest operators combine them with behavioural signals rather than relying on demographics alone.
The Ideal Customer Profile and the buyer persona

Practitioners distinguish two targeting layers. The Ideal Customer Profile, or ICP, describes the organisation or household that gets the most value from an offering and has the budget and authority to buy — a company-level construct built from firmographic, technographic, and behavioural characteristics. The buyer persona then profiles the individual decision-maker inside that account. They are sequential layers, not interchangeable.

The discipline pays: 71% of companies that exceed revenue goals maintain documented buyer personas, and Salesforce research finds 86% of business buyers are more likely to buy when a seller demonstrably understands their goals.
Jobs to Be Done: target the job, not the demographic

Harvard Business School's Clayton Christensen argued that customers "hire" a product to do a job and "fire" it when it does the job poorly — so the unit of analysis should be the job, not the customer's demographic profile.

Christensen's canonical example is a developer selling condominiums to downsizing retirees. Sales were weak until the company realised its real business was not construction but "transitioning lives" — and added services to help buyers move and decide what to keep or discard. After that, sales took off.
The lesson generalises: most innovation efforts fail, Christensen argued, because teams over-index on customer profiles and data correlations instead of the causal motivation behind a purchase.
Intent signals and data-driven targeting

Modern targeting increasingly runs on intent data — digital traces of purchase interest. These split into "known" first-party signals tied to identified individuals: demo requests, content downloads, webinar sign-ups captured in a CRM. And "anonymised" second- and third-party signals: aggregated site-visitor data, research-report trends, social listening.
Intent data lets sellers reach in-market accounts before they self-identify. Chapter 4 shows an industrial manufacturer converting this into a $181M pipeline in a single quarter.
In practice
Define the account-level ICP first, then the individual persona, then organise the offer around the job the customer is hiring you to do.
Validate targets with behavioural intent signals rather than demographics alone.
Treat "goal understanding" as a conversion lever in its own right — 86% of B2B buyers reward it.
